results.title: The Best Approach Shoes | Tested & Ranked
results.description: Rock climbers need special sticky rubber shoes on the wall, but it helps to have a technical shoe with strategically placed rubber for approaching the...
extra_snippets: Rock climbers need special sticky rubber shoes on the wall, but it helps to have a technical shoe with strategically placed rubber for approaching the climb, too. If you typically hike miles with a heavy pack to approach your climbing objectives, or find yourself scrambling up rugged terrain, you understand what we mean.

In this review, we compare eight of the best models on the market today in side-by-side tests. We used the shoes to ascend fixed lines, hike with heavy packs, and traverse knife-edge ridges. All the while, our experienced testers took careful notes and compared performance. Our testers chose the La Sportiva TX Guide as the best approach shoes.

If you're interested in climbing ropes or looking for a new climbing harness, we've got you covered. We have also tested just about all the top belay devices on the market. Related: Best Approach Shoes for Women

Our favorite approach shoe is the La Sportiva TX Guide. This shoe can do it all, from climbing moderate rock pitches to hiking through rugged terrain and aid climbing on big walls. It is supportive, lightweight, and precise, excelling in every movement test we threw at it.

results.title: 4 Best Approach Shoes of 2026 (Tested & reviewed)
results.description: We test approach shoes to scramble to your climbing base - grip, traction, sticky rubber, compact, lightweight and support/comfort for long hikes and gear hauls
extra_snippets: Approach shoes are a hybrid between hiking shoes and climbing footwear—they offer the grip of a climbing shoe and the comfort of a hiker, so you can move confidently over rocky, exposed terrain en route to the crag.

We tested a range of approach shoes in real climbing settings—from Yosemite and the High Sierra to Squamish, BC—evaluating how each performed during multipitch transitions, technical scrambles, long slab ascents and descents, and casual moves across forest trails, high alpine terrain, loose rock, and boulder fields.

The TX2 EVOs are the best all-around approach shoes we tried. These shoes offer excellent traction and sensitivity while preserving comfort and support. For any short to intermediate distance the support is perfectly adequate. Where the TX2s really shine is on the rock.

Climbing up and down, these gave the closest “climbing shoe” control on technical scrambling up talus fields and down crack systems. Add in packability for a multipitch, a wide toe box, and the option to resole when rubber wears thin, and you have an amazing all-around approach shoe!

results.title: Approach Boots for Hiking Guide: How to Choose the Right Pair
results.description: Learn when approach boots outperform hiking shoes, key features to consider, and how to pick the best option for rocky, off-trail terrain.
extra_snippets: If you're hiking technical trails with scrambling, loose rock, or steep slabs, approach boots are often better than traditional hiking boots. They combine sticky rubber soles from climbing shoes with durable uppers from hiking footwear, offering superior grip and precision on rugged terrain.

Recently, advancements in breathable waterproof membranes and midsole stiffness have made them viable even in wet conditions. If you’re a typical user tackling mixed terrain—especially off-trail sections—you don’t need to overthink this: an approach boot like the La Sportiva TX5 or Arc'teryx Vertex Alpine GTX will likely serve you better than a standard hiking shoe.

They are not intended for sustained mountaineering or ice travel but work well in dry to moderately damp alpine settings. According to Wikipedia, approach shoes are “hybrid footwear” meant for both trail use and technical terrain, particularly where standard hiking footwear lacks grip 1.

This reduces effectiveness in technical terrain. If you want a daily-wear shoe with some trail capability, consider trail runners or lightweight hiking shoes instead. Approach shoes prioritize rock grip and precision with sticky rubber and a climbing zone, making them suitable for scrambling.

results.title: My Go-To 4-Shoe Running Rotation - by Brittany Vermeer
results.description: Running shoe preference is a very individual thing. Some runners like max cushion, while others prefer a minimalist approach with more ground feel.
extra_snippets: Apparently I'm a Nike/Saucony kind of girl. These are my four favorite running shoes for triathlon.

For athletes running multiple days a week, you need more than one training shoe. This is because you have to give the foam some time to bounce back from your previous session- 24 hours at a minimum. We never run in the same shoe on back-to-back days.
So, today we’ll cover a few running shoe best practices, and I’ll share my go-to 4-shoe running rotation.
One time, I was chatting with an older athlete who does Ironman and 70.3 races. She told me that she only has one pair of shoes…

results.title: Why Approach Shoes are the best Mountain Footwear - Backpacker's Mentality
results.description: When you’re planning a mountain ... shoes and suffer through the approach? There’s a better answer for mountain adventures… Read More »Why Approach Shoes are the best Mountain Footwear...
extra_snippets: When you’re planning a mountain adventure that involves more than just walking on established trails, you face a dilemma: do you wear hiking boots and struggle on technical terrain, go for trail runners for ligthweigth comfort and avoid techncial terrian, or climbing shoes and suffer through the approach?

There’s a better answer for mountain adventures and they’e called approach shoes. So let’s explore why Approach Shoes are the ultimate weapon to conquer the moutnains from day hikes to long distance trekking to rock climbing. First we will learn the difference in moutain footwear, why approach shoes are special, especially if your trails mix rock, scree, and steep terrain.

For years, there has been a fierce debate amongst the hiking, climbing and backpacking communities about the best footwear to use. I was in the mountain shoe/boot camp in my youth, casting a vote for the more rigid and traditional approach with a low cut due to my preference for ankle flexibility.

Rock climbing shoes offer incredible precision and grip on stone, but are uncomfortable for anything beyond short approaches. Try hiking several miles in climbing shoes, and your feet will quickly remind you they weren’t designed for walking. To add in confusion, trail running shoes have exploded in popularity thanks to being lighter and often more cushioned than hiking boots.

results.title: Sticky Rubber, Trail Comfort, Crag Style: 6 Approach Shoes We Swear By
results.description: Tested on long hikes, sketchy scrambles, and alpine missions—these are the approach shoes that can do it all.
extra_snippets: We tested 26 approach shoes. These are the best for long alpine scrambles, car-to-crag jaunts, and everything in between.

The best rock climbing shoes in the world aren’t going to do you any good if you can’t reach your route in the first place. Sure, some climbs are a stone’s throw from the car, but others require fourth or fifth-class scrambling to reach the first pitch. And even when a climb does have an easy approach, the descent could involve rappelling, scrambling, downclimbing, or several miles of hiking.

Tester and Climbing magazine’s Senior Editor Anthony Walsh calls this model “a one-shoe wonder, threading the needle between runner and approach shoe.” The Vibram XS Flash 2 sole is built for edging, and also has a flat, smearable toe.

The shoe is made with a Matryx upper, which is made with high-tenacity polyester coated with polyurethane and a blend of technical yarns like aramid fiber and Kevlar. Our test models showed minimal signs of wear after 100-plus miles, taking on everything from friction-y 5.8 alpine scrambles to 30-mile hiking approaches.

results.title: Approach Shoes Vs Hiking Boots Are Hiking Boots Still Worth It
results.description: Approach shoes vs hiking boots: Are hiking boots still worth it? Compare performance, comfort, and durability to choose the right footwear for your adventures.
extra_snippets: Alibaba Sportswear & Outdoor Apparel Sports & Outdoor Shoes Hiking Shoes ... For decades, hiking boots have been the go-to footwear for outdoor enthusiasts—trusted for their durability, ankle support, and rugged protection on rough terrain. But in recent years, a new contender has emerged: approach shoes.

Designed originally for climbers transitioning from trail to rock, these hybrid shoes blend the agility of trail runners with the grip and structure of hiking footwear. As more hikers opt for lighter, faster gear, the question arises: Are traditional hiking boots still worth it? The answer isn’t black and white. It depends on your terrain, load, experience level, and personal preferences. Understanding the strengths and limitations of both options is key to making an informed decision. At first glance, approach shoes and hiking boots may seem similar—both are built for off-road use and feature aggressive outsoles.

They resemble athletic shoes with sticky rubber soles optimized for scrambling and short hikes, often used by climbers accessing routes. The choice between them ultimately comes down to trade-offs: support versus speed, protection versus agility. Tip: If you frequently scramble over rocks or carry light packs, approach shoes may offer superior comfort and responsiveness.

This comparison reveals that hiking boots remain unmatched when carrying heavy backpacks, traversing unstable alpine zones, or hiking through wet environments. Approach shoes shine when speed and dexterity matter more than brute protection—such as summit approaches, via ferratas, or day hikes with minimal gear.

results.title: Why does every shoe look like an approach shoe now?
results.description: Footwear designed for all sorts of things are borrowing design queues from approach shoes, creating a wild storm of GORP-y lookalikes, a swirl of wrap-rubber, toe caps, and eyelets that lash laces from talus to toe.
extra_snippets: Because an “approach” in climbing can involve anything from a brief walk to a roadside crag to a full-on expedition, the category houses a lot of variation. For simplicity’s sake, we’ll look at the anatomy of the TX4, using my own clapped-out pair (which I got used from Second Wind Sports in Bozeman in 2020) as a model. Pardon my dirty shoe.

A sturdy rubber rand (which runs around the base of the shoe) for abrasion resistance and protection in sharp terrain. A stiff (usually) cushioned midsole that protects your foot on uneven ground and supports edging. A climbing rubber outsole, but with lugs for traction when not on rock. A reinforced toe cap for durability and protection as you step, smear, jam, etc. As you can see, the design of an approach shoe clearly derives from its specific function: those that look like the TX4 are made to hike and climb.

If you live in a city and want to signal that you’re a climber, wearing a pair of TX4s is a heck of a lot more comfortable than tip-toeing around in your actual climbing shoes. And it helps that most approach shoes have a certain visual appeal, one that’s edgier than most hiking footwear and that I attribute primarily to contrast: blocky soles with curved/anatomical uppers; long tongues with stubby toe caps; thick laces with thin tension loops; smooth rubber that cuts a line along roughened suede.

On the functional side, approach shoes also work decently well for city living, or so the loudest voices of r/hiking say. More than one user has called them “the perfect urban shoe.” Others point to their grip, durability, comfortability, and “reasonable” good looks.

results.title: The Wrong Shoe theory: Why your footwear shouldn't match your outfit - The Times of India
results.description: We often fall into the trap of thinking ethnic wear must be paired with ethnic shoes. The Wrong Shoe Theory asks you to break that rule.The Saree Update: We usually default to heels or embellished wedges with a saree. But have you tried the "Sonam Kapoor approach"?
extra_snippets: Have you ever put together a look that made perfect sense on paper but felt incredibly boring in the mirror? You wore the floral kurta with the matchi.

You did everything "right," yet the result looked more like a mannequin display than a personal style statement.If this sounds familiar, your problem might be that your shoes match your outfit too well.Enter the "Wrong Shoe Theory," a styling trick that has quietly taken over the fashion world - from the streets of Milan to the hustle of Mumbai.

The concept is simple. Instead of finishing a look with the obvious footwear choice, you intentionally choose the shoe that opposes the vibe of your clothes. If your outfit is feminine and flowery, you skip the delicate sandals and reach for chunky sneakers.

Let’s be real, this trend is a blessing for anyone navigating the Delhi Metro or a Mumbai local. We’ve been told for years that "beauty is pain," but the Wrong Shoe Theory argues that comfort is actually cooler.

results.title: 10 Best Approach Shoes For Hiking & Climbing (2026)
results.description: Discover the 10 best approach shoes tested for scrambling, hiking, and alpine terrain, with real-world performance on rock, trail, and long approaches.
extra_snippets: I needed something in between, shoes that could handle a 10-mile16 kilometer day with a pack and still feel confident on low 5th class rock. I’ve tested 15+ models across Serbia’s Tara National Park, the Bulgarian Rila Mountains, and Romania’s Bucegi Mountains since then. Here’s what I got. The TX4 nails the balance between technical scrambling and long-distance hiking better than any other approach shoe I’ve tested.

The suede leather upper runs warm in summer heat, which is worth noting if you’re hiking in hot climates. But that same leather construction is why these boots outlast mesh alternatives by years. If you need one shoe that does everything reasonably well, start here. For more details of this approach shoe, check out our review of the La Sportiva TX4 Evo.

But that same leather, combined with the 1.5mm TechLite rand wrapping the shoe, means you can jam your foot into cracks, scrape across granite slabs, and generally abuse them in ways that would shred a mesh shoe in a season. The Vibram Megagrip sole, with its smooth climbing zone under the toe, handles 4th class rock with the kind of confidence that lets you actually look at the view instead of your feet. Fit runs slightly large with a wider toe box than typical La Sportiva (I went down a half size). And they weigh around 1 lb 8 oz680 gram per pair, which you’ll notice if you’re used to trail runners. But on approaches with a 30-pound14 kilogram pack, the injected EVA midsole provides enough cushion that my knees aren’t screaming by mile eight.

The Rapid XT sits in that middle ground where a lot of shoes get mediocre at everything. Somehow Scarpa made it work. Three weeks across the Julian Alps put them through via ferrata one day, and a 15-mile24 kilometer trail approach the next, and they handled both without complaint.

Every detail is studied to ensure grip, comfort and protection such as the all-around TPU rand, which is abrasion resistant. The sole is Vibram® MegaGrip with climbing zones at the toe and Brake System in the back, ensuring stability and grip.

Scarpa shoes and boots are built to the highest standard and should serve you for many years of use.
However, just like tyres on a car, the rubber parts of a boot, shoe or rock shoe can wear down with use, and that’s why Scarpa offer a full re-sole service.

results.title: Switchback Travel | Best Approach Shoes
results.description: See our guide to the best approach shoes, with top men’s and women’s shoes for hiking and scrambling from La Sportiva, Scarpa, Black Diamond, and more.
extra_snippets: From lightweight models to tough footwear for technical climbing and the alpine, we break down the year’s top approach shoes

Combining the features of hiking shoes, climbing slippers, and even mountaineering boots, approach shoes are in a category of their own. For climbers, they’re an essential piece of footwear that provides stability and traction on rock in addition to comfort for miles on the trail.

And because approaches can take on so many different forms—from walking to the crag to scrambling fourth or fifth class or embarking on a multi-day slog—approach shoes are a diverse bunch. Below we break down the top approach shoes, including comfortable crag and trail shoes, nimble climbers, and mid-height waterproof models.

They’re narrower, too, which may be a dealbreaker for those with particularly high-volume feet (the original model’s wide toe box was a boon for on-trail comfort). But overall, the core DNA of our favorite all-around approach shoe remains—including standout grip, well-rounded performance, and a hardwearing build that will stand the test of time.

Finding the right walking shoe can transform your outdoor adventures from uncomfortable slogs into pure enjoyment. You need something that barely weighs a thing on your feet but grabs onto rocks, roots, and slippery trails like a mountain goat. These specialized approach shoes bridge the gap between lightweight hiking footwear and technical climbing gear, giving you the best of both worlds without compromise.

Your feet will appreciate the breathable mesh upper that keeps things cool during long approaches to climbing routes or challenging day hikes. The low-profile design hugs your foot without feeling restrictive, allowing natural movement while maintaining support on uneven ground. Many users report feeling more connected to the trail compared to bulkier hiking boots. Trail runners often switch to these when conditions get technical because the sticky rubber outperforms standard running shoe treads.

Quick-drying materials mean you can ford streams without sloshing around in soggy shoes for the rest of your hike. Budget-conscious adventurers often find the TX3 offers the best value in the La Sportiva approach line, delivering performance that punches above its price point without noticeable compromises in quality or capability.

results.title: We walked miles to test the best hiking shoes 2026 | TGO Magazine
results.description: Not so long ago, it was taken as ... a little in recent years, however, with some people starting to move towards a lighter, nimbler approach to footwear and choosing hiking shoes, walking shoes or trail running shoes instead....
extra_snippets: Not so long ago, it was taken as gospel that every hiker needed a big pair of solid, chunky hiking boot to help them stomp over any terrain. Things have changed a little in recent years, however, with some people starting to move towards a lighter, nimbler approach to footwear and choosing hiking shoes, walking shoes or trail running shoes instead.
We’ve also added some budget hiking shoes into the mix here. This is a truly excellent quality approach and hiking shoe that will deliver comfort and confidence for years to come.

The Rapid XT are a non-waterproof option (there is a Rapid XT GTX with Gore-Tex membrane) and are classed as an approach shoe, making them ideal for rough walk-ins on scrambles and climbs, but also aimed at hiking and trail use. A sturdily built 1.8mm suede leather upper is paired with a generous, tough and protective rand that stretches around the whole shoe.

The flexibility of the shoe means it’s best suited to those confident in their movement on more challenging terrain, or else kept to well-established trails and paths. Read more: Lara’s full Salomon Genesis Gore-Tex review · The suede outer may give these an approach, or casual outdoor shoe look, but in use the Ultras feel like a tough running shoe.

results.title: Dakota Johnson Makes a Case for Sensible Shoes in Celebrity Street Style for Spring 2026 Fashion
results.description: Johnson kept her palette neutral. Black jeans created a streamlined base. A roomy sweater softened the sharpness of the shoes. A structured coat added polish. Accessories were minimal but deliberate. This approach answers one of the most searched style questions around sensible footwear.
extra_snippets: Dakota Johnson's latest celebrity street style appearance spotlights a practical Derby shoe that is quickly becoming a key trend in Spring 2026 fashion.

When it comes to celebrity street style, Dakota Johnson has built a reputation for choosing pieces that feel realistic rather than theatrical. Her latest appearance in New York is already feeding into early conversations about Spring 2026 fashion, thanks to one surprisingly practical shoe choice.

According to a recent report from Vogue, Johnson stepped out in a pair of sturdy lace-up Derby shoes by Legres, pairing them with straight-leg black jeans, a relaxed knit, and a tailored coat. The look was understated, functional, and polished without trying too hard.

The Derby shoe is not new. Traditionally rooted in menswear, it is defined by its closed lacing system, low heel, and sturdy sole. What feels current is how it is being styled. Johnson's version reads less academic and more quietly cool.

It’s best suited for moderate to technical trails, road-to-trail use, and longer efforts where comfort and durability are more important than speed. Our in-depth review of the Teva Aventrail Shoe, a closed-toed version of Teva's trail running platform with straps instead of laces.

I tested the Aventrail across technical trails, mud, smooth singletrack, gravel, and a few miles of road, and in every scenario it behaved far more like a traditional trail shoe than its unconventional appearance suggests.

CHAD: No, your eyes aren’t deceiving you, your favorite Clydesdale shoe reviewer is now officially moonlighting in the Dirt Division! The year 2026 for me is all about trying new things, which includes getting into trail running and running my first 50K. I hope you all enjoy my part-time transition as much as I have!

The promise of a shoe that can handle everything from moderate hikes to challenging climbing sections immediately piqued my interest. I was searching for a reliable approach shoe that would hold up to the rigors of multi-day backpacking trips with off-trail scrambling and some easy climbing.

I chose the La Sportiva TX Guide Leather Approach Shoes – Men’s Leather because of La Sportiva’s reputation for quality climbing footwear and the appealing combination of leather construction with a sticky Vibram outsole.
